issue_title: "Bit length" vs. "symbol length"
issue_body: It strikes me as odd that the smallest temporal unit in a signal is referred to as a "bit" when technically it's a symbol. Unless the modulation index is 2 (e.g. BPSK or OOK), there may be more than one bit represented by one signal state.

I generate a loader with the following command line:

But I can not compile the resulting file because `GLintptr` and `GLsizeiptr` are not defined. This bug appeared between version 0.1.13a and 0.1.14a. I think it may be related to the change of registry in commit 2e24db13434d2a802d8126549880f434e618a7bb.

issue_title: Property of GLAD_INCLUDE_DIRS is private
issue_body:
Since I wanted to use glad as subporject of my project, I wrote this code
```cmake
add_subdirectory(extern/glad) 
...
target_link_libraries(${PROJECT_NAME}
  OpenGL::GL
  glfw
  glad
...
```
However, *GLAD_INCLUDE_DIRS* of *glad* target is set to PRIVATE so that my compiler couldn't find *glad/glad.h*.

I just wonder why *GLAD_INCLUDE_DIRS* is set to private
Is there any reason for this?
https://github.com/Dav1dde/glad/blob/ec01ac515dba0730dca375887bb1bd60bc92013c/CMakeLists.txt#L84
To use *glad* target in other cmake project, I think *GLAD_INCLUDE_DIRS* should be set to PUBLIC not PRIVATE.

issue_title: Paralogous genes (without introns) stealing mapping in splice mode
issue_body:
We've been looking at the recent mapping results of a MinION cDNA run, and discovered a few genes that were curiously absent in minimap2-generated files. These were genes that had high expression (according to STAR) in Illumina reads from the same cell line.

I dug into the details of one of these genes ([Eno1](http://asia.ensembl.org/Mus_musculus/Gene/Compara_Paralog?db=core;g=ENSMUSG00000063524;r=4:150236721-150248879;t=ENSMUST00000080926)), and discovered that it had an intron-less paralog (with >99% identity and coverage) in the mus musculus genome. Mapping a minion read separately to both regions produced positive matches in both cases, but only one match was returned when the regions were combined as a multi-fasta file. This issue remains when reducing down to a minimal test case containing just the two genes of interest, but disappears when I remove the `-p 10` mode from minimap2.

While I don't expect minimap2 to prioritise the spliced mapping over the non-spliced mapping, it'd be nice if it included the spliced mapping as at least a secondary mapping.

issue_title: Likelihood NaN
issue_body:
Likelihood fitting does not always work, it quite easely results in NaN. This can be caused by giving poor initial conditions, but also has more fundamental reasons. In particular I would preffer to use likelihood rather than loglikelihood because likelihood is always a number between 0 and 1, whereas loglikelihood can be between -inf and 0, which might be problematic.

Currently, the following is minimised:
`ans = - np.nansum(np.log(func(x, p)))`
Thereby maximizing the loglikelihood. I would prefer to minimize the following:
`ans = - np.product(func(x, p))`
However, this does not work nearly as good. How come?

issue_title: S3 Event Source delete function not working as expected
issue_body:
The S3 Event Source delete function is not working as expected. This is tangentially related to issue https://github.com/garnaat/kappa/issues/88. The delete function expects that only one notification has been set up. It checks the first entry in"CloudFunctionConfiguration" to see if the function arn matches. If it does, it removes the whole "CloudFunctionConfiguration" block. 

This in incorrect for two reasons:
-  If a different notification has been set up and appears before the expected notification, the expected notification will not be deleted.
-  If a different notification has been set up and appears after the expected notification, the expected notification and the other notification will both be deleted. 

EDIT:
It's not that the code checks for the first entry in "CloudFunctionConfiguration" it's that it uses the deprecated [get-bucket-notification](http://docs.aws.amazon.com/cli/latest/reference/s3api/get-bucket-notification.html) which only returns the first notification. The result is still the same.

issue_title: Boolean values in PdfDict
issue_body:
I wanted to set ViewerPreferences in my output so as to get: `ViewerPreferences <</FitWindow true>>`.

I first tried: 
```
writer.trailer.Root.ViewerPreferences = PdfDict(FitWindow=True)
```
But this produces `True` instead of `true` in the output. And of course, `FitWindow='true'` gives `(true)`.

I looked at the code but didn't find any obvious clues. I finally got it working with the following kludge:
```
class PdfTrue(object):
    def __str__(self):
        return "true"

writer.trailer.Root.ViewerPreferences = PdfDict(FitWindow=PdfTrue())
```

I feel there should be a more canonical way to achieve this.

issue_title: Update ETA more frequently than the bar
issue_body:
The following example shows a default progress bar that takes a long time to complete. The problem is that the ETA value is only updated when the progress bar advances:

```
import pyprind
import time
amount = 80000
bar = pyprind.ProgBar(amount, width=10)
for i in enumerate(range(amount)):
  time.sleep(.1) # some op
  bar.update()
```

issue_title: ldap3.add modifies the passed in values
issue_body:
When you have code like this:

```python
conn = ldap3.Connection(....)
initial = {'attr': [value, value], 'other_attr': [value, value]}
print(initial)
conn.add('dn', [list, of, object, Classes], initial)
print(initial)
```

The expectation would be that the two `print` statements result in identical output. However, when passing my dict of attributes into `add` that dict gets modified by the add operation to include the object classes I passed in as a list (the second argument).

This showed up in some of my test cases where I compiled an initial dict of attributes, did the add and after the add had it log that an entity was created with `initial.keys()`. At that point my tests started to fail b/c  `sorted(initial.keys())` before and after the add operation were no longer the same.

Imho this shouldn't happen. If `ldap3.add` goes about and changes the dict I push into it, it should use `copy.deepcopy()` to create its own since there's no way for ldap3 to know if I might be using that dict somewhere else too, or access it later. I've fixed this in my own code by doing an `add(... copy.deepcopy(inital))` as a workaround.

issue_title: Mlp is not just noncopyable, but also nonmovable
issue_body:

issue_title: Tests failing randomly
issue_body:
The tests are failing since the introduction of the `--progress` support. The issue being inconsistent failing of the same code between calls. Cf the [travis reports](https://travis-ci.org/guyzmo/git-repo/builds/129130635).

With a fair bit of debugging, I found out the issue is that because GitPython relies on the `kwargs` construct to pass around optional arguments, the order of argument will depend on the order of the items within the dict. As when I introduced progress support, `opt_arg` contains `'-v'` and `'--progress'` now, the order depending on both `hash('-v')` and `hash('--progress')`. To fix this, making the optional arguments ordered on their value instead on their hash will do the job, but sadly it's not something that can be fixed with a tiny bit of monkey patching…

Thus, I've opened a PR upstream at gitpython-developers/GitPython#431 with a very simple fix for that.

    else:
        not_done = False

```
It seems that loop breaks when ```remote_conn.recv_ready() == False```

As a result we stop reading data in case when after one second (by default) we don't get all the data from remote device.

One second is enough in most cases, but if run a command that need more time to be executed,  ```show run``` on Cisco IOS for example, in approximately 50% cases i got only
```
Load for five secs: 26%/0%; one minute: 15%; five minutes: 15%
Time source is NTP, 14:03:28.766 MSK Sat May 30 2015

Building configuration...
```
instead of full output.

issue_title: Humidity Sensor
issue_body:
Uses the humidity sensor scaling for the older sensorTag instead of scaling for HTC1000 used in newer CC2650 sensorTag. Could keep track of which sensorTag is in use and use the appropriate scaling.

SHT21 (old sensorTag)
humidity =-6+125*raw/65536
temperature =-4686 + 175.72*raw/65536

HDC1000 (CC2650 sensorTag)
humidity = (raw/65536)*100
temperature = (raw/65536)*165 - 40

Note: at least one of these values should also have some status bits masked off and attention should be paid to data types and rounding.

issue_title: Performance degradation over time
issue_body:
I noticed that the more you use jedi, the slower it gets (within the same process, speed issue disappear if you start another jedi process). So I crafted a small script with direct calls to jedi API and profiled the first and the last call to jedi. 

The first iteration takes 50ms while the 50th iteration takes 500ms!

I debugged it a bit and it seems like there are duplicated child nodes. E.g. for str, we have a total of 21 child nodes the first time ``deep_ast_copy`` is called, next iteration will have 24 child nodes, next iteration 27 child nodes, and so on...

```python
import jedi
import cProfile
import jedi.debug
import jedi.settings

jedi.debug.debug_function = print
jedi.debug.enable_speed = True


for i in range(50):
    if i == 0 or i == 49:
        cProfile.run("import jedi; s = jedi.Script(''); completions = s.completions()", sort=1)
    else:
        s = jedi.Script('')
        completions = s.completions()
        print('----------------------------------------------------------')
```

issue_title: as_dict does not return pure dicts
issue_body:
Hi there,

I recently started to use psutil - and it is awesome 👍.
Especially the as_dict function is very useful. I try to benchmark some functions and I call the as_dict, bound on the process before and after the function to get my measurements. As the as_dict returns a dict, I wanted to store it via dataset [dataset.readthedocs.io/en/latest/] in a inmemory sqlite database, so that I can write it to disk after the benchmark and do not interfere with the disk I/O. However, dataset only accepts true dicts, while as_dict gives back a dict with named_tuples nested in it (i.e. io_counters, memory_info, memory_full_info, etc) and lists (connections, open_files).
At the moment I am thinking of converting these named_tuples and lists into json so that this degraded dict becomes one which can be handled with sqlite / dataset. Is there anything planed to change the behavior of as_dict or would there be an better way of getting it into some compatible format as I described? :)

Thanks a lot!

issue_title: Error when retrieving net connections
issue_body:
This PR https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/pull/880 causes an issue when connection count can vary during the probe.
I have tracked down the problem to the fact that the GIL is now being released when calling the windows api. For some reason, when the GIL is released, the windows api sometimes returns `ERROR_NOT_ENOUGH_MEMORY`. My only guess is that perhaps it doesn't like having socket changes during the call? It is hard to say for sure since the error is undocumented in the windows api.
In any case, calling 
`psutil.Process().connections(kind="tcp4")`

can sometimes result in 
```
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\__init__.py", line 1153, in connections
    return self._proc.connections(kind)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 635, in wrapper
    return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 917, in connections
    return net_connections(kind, _pid=self.pid)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 325, in net_connections
    rawlist = cext.net_connections(_pid, families, types)
MemoryError
```
or 
```
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\__init__.py", line 1153, in connections
    return self._proc.connections(kind)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 635, in wrapper
    return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 917, in connections
    return net_connections(kind, _pid=self.pid)
  File "d:\open_source\psutil\psutil\_pswindows.py", line 325, in net_connections
    rawlist = cext.net_connections(_pid, families, types)
WindowsError: [Error -1073741823] Windows Error 0xC0000001
```
I've attached the (somewhat messy) test to reproduce the issue.
[test_psutil.py.txt](https://github.com/giampaolo/psutil/files/2094424/test_psutil.py.txt)

I understand the reasoning behind releasing the GIL in the loop (and you had mentioned it in the PR), so not sure how you wanted to handle this.

Thanks

issue_title: KafkaConsumer Fails to Report Problem with Compression
issue_body:
When no module exists to handle Snappy decompression, the KafkaConsumer returns no messages, rather than reporting the problem.  This differs from the legacy Consumer API which provides a much more useful error message.

### Background

I was attempting to fetch some data from a Kafka topic which was using snappy compression.  No data was ever returned even though I knew data was being landed in the topic (confirmed with the Kafka CLI tools).  This had me very confused.

```
>>> consumer = kafka.KafkaConsumer("test", bootstrap_servers=["svr:9092"])
>>> consumer.poll(5000)
{}
```

I then attempted to use the legacy consumer API which pointed me to the exact problem.  
```
>>> client = kafka.SimpleClient("svr:9092")
>>> consumer.close()
>>> consumer = kafka.SimpleConsumer(client, "group", "test")
>>> for message in consumer:
...     print(message)
...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>

issue_title: Traceback not sent to logger, but directly to stderr
issue_body:
https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/b1ae45c10d46e881492b0fd37f0919cac6d79224/kafka/conn.py#L618

At the line above, BrokerConnection prints a traceback directly to stderr, ignoring the logger. This makes it hard to filter out the traceback. Right now, for me the traceback occasionally starts generating GBs of output while at the same time a Producer stops sending information to Kafka, although why this happens I'm not sure yet, and I don't yet have a minimal example (I think it has something to do with losing a network connection though).

I think a better implementation would probably be to remove the traceback, and add stack_info=True to the previous logging call, as I think it does roughly the same thing. Or just get rid of the traceback, if it's not providing value to the project.

I should be able to provide a pull request, but I don't know if it would be preferable to get rid of the traceback entirely or not.

issue_body:
General informations:

* OS name: __Windows__
* OS version: __8.1__
* OS architecture: __64 bits__
* Monitor(s)'s resolution: __Monitor 1: 1600x900 ; Monitor 2: 1920x1080__
* Result of the command `python --version`: __Python 2.7.9__

### Description of the warning/error

The content of mss().monitors is incorrect:

```
import mss
for monitor in mss.mss().monitors:
	print monitor
```

Return: 
```
{'width': 2880, 'top': 0, 'height': 900, 'scale': 1.0, 'left': 0}
{'width': 1600, 'top': 0, 'height': 900, 'scale': 1.0, 'left': 0}
{'width': 1280, 'top': 0, 'height': 720, 'scale': 1.0, 'left': 1600}
```
The third line does not correspond with the secondary monitor resolution.

### Full message
Here is a testing script I made:
```
import mss
import mss.tools
from Tkinter import *
app = Tk()

def screenshot():
	top = btn.winfo_rooty()
	left = btn.winfo_rootx()

	mss.tools.to_png(sct_img.rgb, sct_img.size, output="screenshot.png")

btn.pack(fill=X,padx=50, pady=50)

print "Monitors:"
for monitor in mss.mss().monitors:
	print monitor

app.mainloop()
```
The script creates a simple Tkinter windows with a buton. A click on the buton save a screenshot of that button, using the coordinate provided by Tkinter.

The script works just fine on Ubuntu.

### Environment

Windows 7 64 bit, Python 3.6, pyodbc 4.0.2x (I tried a few of them) 

### Issue

Expected: If I create a temp table in one call, don't close the connection and make a second call, the temp table should stick around and be available for the subsequent call.

This works if parameters are not used. As soon as I use a parameter, all bets are off.

#This version works:

conn = pyodbc.connect("DRIVER={SQL Server};SERVER=server;PORT=1433;Trusted_Connection=yes;")
cur = conn.cursor()

issue_title: Camel Case in module name

issue_title: piano_roll_integrated error in class Instrument get_piano_roll method if fs >= 10
issue_body:
`python 2.7.13`, `pretty_midi 0.2.8`

When we use `get_chroma()` method in class `PrettyMIDI`, if we set parameter `fs` >= 10, the chromagram matrix we get sometimes contains `NaN` column. And with `numpy` warning:
```
/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/core/_methods.py:59: RuntimeWarning: Mean of empty slice.
  warnings.warn("Mean of empty slice.", RuntimeWarning)
/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/numpy/core/_methods.py:68: RuntimeWarning: invalid value encountered in true_divide
  ret, rcount, out=ret, casting='unsafe', subok=False)
```

Traceback this issue, the problem is under `get_piano_roll()` method in class `Instrument`.  Here I show the last several lines above method `return`:
```python
if times is None:
            return piano_roll
piano_roll_integrated = np.zeros((128, times.shape[0]))
# Convert to column indices
times = np.array(times*fs, dtype=np.int)
for n, (start, end) in enumerate(zip(times[:-1], times[1:])):
    # Each column is the mean of the columns in piano_roll
    piano_roll_integrated[:, n] = np.mean(piano_roll[:, start:end],axis=1)
return piano_roll_integrated
```
When we compute `piano_roll_integrated`, we use `np.mean` of `start:end`. But if `start` and `end` are in same value, the `length` of `start:end` will be `zero`. 

Now lets look at why it is possible `start` and `end` value is possible to be same with parameter `fs` = 10. Here I abstract a small part of `numpy` test, but for this case, it is exactly true values in my project.
```python
import numpy as np
times = np.arange(5., 85, 0.1)
print times
times2 = np.array(times * 10, dtype=np.int)
print times2
print np.size(times), np.size(times2)
```
We can get the `times2` result is `[ 50  51  51  52  53  54  55  56  57  58 ...`. There are two `51`s in index 1 and index 2. Which cause problems.

This is because of the precision of number 52.0 is `51.999999999999993`, when using `dtype=np.int`, it ignores decimal part and equal to `51`.

To fix this issue, we can simply change it like this:
```python
import numpy as np
times = np.arange(5., 85, 0.1)
print times
times2 = np.array(np.round(times * 10), dtype=np.int)
print (times * 10)[2]
print times2
print np.size(times), np.size(times2)
```
As a result, `times2` will be a `no-duplicate` sequence.

Reflect to `pretty_midi`, I think we can fix like this:
```python
        if times is None:
            return piano_roll
        piano_roll_integrated = np.zeros((128, times.shape[0]))
        # Convert to column indices
        times = np.array(np.round(times*fs), dtype=np.int)
        for n, (start, end) in enumerate(zip(times[:-1], times[1:])):
            # Each column is the mean of the columns in piano_roll
            piano_roll_integrated[:, n] = np.mean(piano_roll[:, start:end],axis=1)
        return piano_roll_integrated
```
I did test on my music midi to chroma convert, the problem has solved.

issue_title: Doesn't work with Python3 due to unicode()
issue_body: Currently this doesn't work with python 3 because unicode() was remade to str() in python3. If you want to have it work with 2 and 3, check http://python-future.org/compatible_idioms.html#unicode

issue_title: can't commit: b'Resource temporarily unavailable'
issue_body:
Sometimes commit() throws exception if commits aren't far from each other. Looks like it requires refresh(). The same problem if I edit iptables calling the iptables binary before commit() is called.

#BAD
#get chain from table
table = iptc.Table(iptc.Table.NAT)
table.autocommit = False
chain = iptc.Chain(table, 'PREROUTING')
#delete old rules
for rule in chain.rules:
	chain.delete_rule(rule)
#commit updates
table.commit()
#create rule
rule = iptc.Rule()
#set it up
...
#add rule
chain.insert_rule(rule)
#commit updates
table.commit()

#GOOD
#get chain from table
table = iptc.Table(iptc.Table.NAT)
table.refresh()
table.autocommit = False
chain = iptc.Chain(table, 'PREROUTING')
#delete old rules
for rule in chain.rules:
	chain.delete_rule(rule)
#commit updates
table.commit()
table.refresh()
#create rule
rule = iptc.Rule()
#set it up

#commit updates

issue_title: AuxPoW Abstraction
issue_body:
Merge-mined blockchains such as Namecoin and Dogecoin use a different proof of work scheme (AuxPoW), in which a block header and coinbase transaction from a parent chain (usually Bitcoin or Litecoin) is included in the Namecoin or Dogecoin block header.  This extra data (referred to as an AuxPoW header) is required in order to verify the PoW (meaning that SPV cannot work without this data).

As far as I can tell, ElectrumX does not currently support sending AuxPoW headers when it sends block headers to a client.  As a result, ElectrumX servers cannot be used with Electrum clients that validate AuxPoW, which makes ElectrumX's support for Namecoin and Dogecoin basically useless unless the client fully trusts the server to serve the chain with the most work.

Is there any chance this could be added?  I'm not too familiar with the ElectrumX codebase, but at first glance it looks like overriding the [`electrum_header` method](https://github.com/kyuupichan/electrumx/blob/7b8119d78749c30df521bd652fcd960c66b01e4a/lib/coins.py#L239) might be a reasonable way of doing it.

issue_title: How to apply the gradient color to the checked King square?
issue_body:
```
#! /usr/bin/env python

"""
This module is the execution point of the chess GUI application
named Chess Titan.
"""

import sys

import chess
import chess.svg

from PyQt5.QtCore import pyqtSlot, Qt
from PyQt5.QtSvg import QSvgWidget
from PyQt5.QtWidgets import QApplication, QWidget


class MainWindow(QWidget):
    """
    Create a surface for the chessboard.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        """
        Initialize the chessboard.
        """
        super().__init__()

        self.setWindowTitle("Chess GUI")
        self.setGeometry(300, 300, 800, 800)

        self.widgetSvg = QSvgWidget(parent=self)
        self.widgetSvg.setGeometry(10, 10, 600, 600)

        self.boardSize = min(self.widgetSvg.width(),
                             self.widgetSvg.height())
        self.coordinates = True
        self.margin = 0.05 * self.boardSize if self.coordinates else 0
        self.squareSize = (self.boardSize - 2 * self.margin) / 8.0

        self.board = chess.Board()
        self.pieceToMove = [None, None]

    @pyqtSlot(QWidget)
    def mousePressEvent(self, event):
        """
        Handle left mouse clicks and enable moving chess pieces by
        clicking on a chess piece and then the target square.

        Moves must be made according to the rules of chess because
        illegal moves are suppressed.
        """
        if event.x() <= self.boardSize and event.y() <= self.boardSize:
            if event.buttons() == Qt.LeftButton:
                if self.margin < event.x() < self.boardSize - self.margin and self.margin < event.y() < self.boardSize - self.margin:
                    file = int((event.x() - self.margin) / self.squareSize)
                    rank = 7 - int((event.y() - self.margin) / self.squareSize)
                    square = chess.square(file, rank)
                    piece = self.board.piece_at(square)
                    coordinates = "{}{}".format(chr(file + 97), str(rank + 1))
                    if self.pieceToMove[0] is not None:
                        move = chess.Move.from_uci("{}{}".format(self.pieceToMove[1], coordinates))
                        if move in self.board.legal_moves:
                            self.board.push(move)
                            if self.board.is_check():
                                print("Check!")
                        piece = None
                        coordinates = None
                    self.pieceToMove = [piece, coordinates]
                else:
                    # Envoke the paint event.
                    self.update()
        else:
            QWidget.mousePressEvent(self, event)

    @pyqtSlot(QWidget)
    def paintEvent(self, event):
        """
        Draw a chessboard with the starting position and then redraw
        it for every new move.
        """
        self.boardSvg = chess.svg.board(board=self.board,
                                        size=self.boardSize).encode("UTF-8")
        self.widgetSvg.load(self.boardSvg)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    chessTitan = QApplication(sys.argv)
    window = MainWindow()
    window.show()
    sys.exit(chessTitan.exec_())
```

This code creates an SVG chessboard. It allows the pieces to be moved according to the rules of chess. Use the left mouse button to click on a piece and again the left mouse button to click on the target square. If the move if valid, the piece will move to that square.

Currently, if there is check, I print out "Check!". How can I make that gradient color on the square that the checked King is on (either the White or the Black King)?

issue_title: Bug in helpers/builders.py
issue_body:
`Overview:` if we declare route with class based view with method different from METH_ANY ('*') our endpoint doesn't show up
There is a bug in `helpers/builders.py:43`:
```
if issubclass(route.handler, web.View) and route.method == METH_ANY:
```
`STR:`
1. Create route with class based view:
```
app.router.add_route( method='DELETE',
                                  path='/some_path/',
                                  handler=SubClassOf_web_View,
                                  name='some_name')
```
2. Create doc string for DELETE method:
```
class SubClassOf_web_View(web.View):
    async def delete(self):
       """
       ---
       valid doc string
       """
```
`Excpected:` declared endpoint shows up on api page
`Result`: declared endpoint doesn't show up on api page

`Solution`: replace 
```if issubclass(route.handler, web.View) and route.method == METH_ANY: ```
with 
```if issubclass(route.handler, web.View):```

issue_title: Nicer error message when file can't be opened
Use a non-root logger
issue_body:
It would be nice if watchdog didn't write to the root logger (that is, calling logging.debug), but rather created its own logger (logging.getLogger). That would give application writers more control over the output.

I notice this with inotify_buffer.py especially. While I generally would like the root logger to log at DEBUG in my application, doing so means that my log file gets flooded with "InotifyBuffer" messages when there's a bunch of IO in a watched location. If watchdog had its own logger, I could set the level for that independently.

        event, watch = event_queue.get(block=True, timeout=timeout)
        self.dispatch_event(event, watch)

This code path is not thread safe, as the watch object could get removed by the time the "dispatch_event" method obtains it's lock.

When running into this, it results in a KeyError and the resulting thread dying, thus watchdog no longer monitors a path. I had a traceback of this, but I've misplaced it at the moment, will past it it the next time I see it.

issue_title: "Sending %d bytes": not actually bytes
issue_body:
As @dkg noticed in https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole/issues/127#issuecomment-271090403, the `wormhole send --text=` command is too casual with its description of the size of the message being sent. The text claims we're measuring in bytes, whereas the implementation does a simple `len(text)` on the unicode argument.

The fix will be to change that `len(text)` to `len(text.encode("utf-8"))`, which is not significantly more meaningful, but at least could possibly be accurate if we pretend there's an implicit ".. when encoded as UTF-8" in the message (which is certainly not worth adding for real).

issue_title: anyconfig.load/dump should process config files regardless of the files are compressed or not
changes in AST from Python 3.5 on
issue_body:
Hi Timothy,

some scripts lead to an exception raised in checker.py, method checkCall, line 229 of the latest version of frosted. The exception is:

AttributeError: 'Call' object has no attribute 'starargs'.

One exemplary script is:

`def test(self):
    pass
    
def poll(self):
    poll = test()`

The reason might be, that the ast module does not provide the starargs attribute anymore for the object **Call** since Python 3.5 (see for instance http://greentreesnakes.readthedocs.io/en/latest/nodes.html#Call).

In many cases, the if-condition is already evaluated after the first len-comparison, therefore the call_node.starargs and call_node.kwargs (which also does not exist since Python 3.5) is not evaluated. Do you have an idea how to overcome this problem?

issue_title: Test GetSearchableMailboxes support
issue_body:
88552bcc401d25a92d74760668560a0ced2dfdb1 adds initial support for `GetSearchableMailboxes` support, but I have no way to test the service, as my test server always returns `exchangelib.errors.ErrorAccessDenied: The caller has not assigned any of the RBAC roles requested in the management role header.`.

If you have sufficient privileges on your server, please test the service by running:

```python
c = Credentials('username', 'password')
a = Account(primary_smtp_address=c.username, credentials=c, autodiscover=True)

for item in a.protocol.get_searchable_mailboxes():
    print(item)
```

We may need to implement the `ManagementRole` SOAP header: https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/office/jj191277(v=exchg.150).aspx

issue_title: The _post_save_receiver considering "using" argument as mandatory.
issue_body:
Tested on django-reversion==2.0.7

On my current django project I needed to use django's signals framework.
But during test it I got the error:


```
from django.db.models.signals import post_save
from app.models import TheModel

row = TheModel.objects.last()

post_save.send(TheModel, instance=row)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-7-5bea38b7ce12> in <module>()
----> 1 post_save.send(TheModel, instance=row)

/Users/alkalit/.virtualenvs/project/lib/python2.7/site-packages/django/dispatch/dispatcher.pyc in send(self, sender, **named)
    187 
    188         for receiver in self._live_receivers(sender):
--> 189             response = receiver(signal=self, sender=sender, **named)
    190             responses.append((receiver, response))
    191         return responses

TypeError: _post_save_receiver() takes exactly 3 arguments (2 given)
```

I didn't used signals befor so it took some time to figure out with the error.
The problem is that [`_post_save_receiver`](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/master/reversion/revisions.py#L300) realy want to get more args then I give (suddenly, yeah?). 


```
post_save.send(TheModel, instance=row, using='default')
```

It's would be nice if it was strongly marked in documentation or, best, `default` connection used as default (suddenly).

issue_title: collectstatic in non-debug mode fails with
issue_body:
**Summary:**

When using `django-cas-server`, you have to run `collectstatic` in debug mode before you can run it in non-debug mode .  Otherwise you get the following backtrace:

```
$ python manage.py collectstatic
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "manage.py", line 22, in <module>
    execute_from_command_line(sys.argv)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 364, in execute_from_command_line
    utility.execute()
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 338, in execute
    django.setup()
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/__init__.py", line 27, in setup
    apps.populate(settings.INSTALLED_APPS)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/apps/registry.py", line 108, in populate
    app_config.import_models()
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/apps/config.py", line 202, in import_models
    self.models_module = import_module(models_module_name)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/importlib/__init__.py", line 126, in import_module
    return _bootstrap._gcd_import(name[level:], package, level)
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 994, in _gcd_import
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 971, in _find_and_load
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 955, in _find_and_load_unlocked
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 665, in _load_unlocked
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap_external>", line 678, in exec_module
  File "<frozen importlib._bootstrap>", line 219, in _call_with_frames_removed
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cas_server/models.py", line 13, in <module>
    from .default_settings import settings, SessionStore
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/cas_server/default_settings.py", line 21, in <module>
    CAS_LOGO_URL = static("cas_server/logo.png")
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/contrib/staticfiles/templatetags/staticfiles.py", line 12, in static
    return _static(path)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/templatetags/static.py", line 166, in static
    return StaticNode.handle_simple(path)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/templatetags/static.py", line 117, in handle_simple
    return staticfiles_storage.url(path)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py", line 162, in url
    return self._url(self.stored_name, name, force)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py", line 141, in _url
    hashed_name = hashed_name_func(*args)
  File "<venv path>/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py", line 432, in stored_name
    raise ValueError("Missing staticfiles manifest entry for '%s'" % clean_name)
ValueError: Missing staticfiles manifest entry for 'cas_server/logo.png'
```

**Analysis:**

When `django-cas-server` module gets loaded, its default settings use the template tag `static` to find out where its default logo is.  In debug mode, this function just returns the path to the file without any other faff. [[1]](https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/contrib/staticfiles/storage.py#L117)

However, in non-debug mode, it tries to search the staticfiles manifest for the file.  If there is no manifest because `collectstatic` has not been run before, this fails.

**Workaround:**

At the moment you can work around this by running `collectstatic` in debug mode before turning it off.  This isn't ideal in production though.

        z = y1[i]
        for inp in range (n_inputs):
            z -= np.dot (alphas[inp,:], inputs[inp, i-M/2,i+M/2+1])

        for inp in range (n_inputs):
            for j in range (M):
                w = inputs[inp,i-M/2+j]
                errs[inp,j] += z * w.conjugate()

        if i % chunksize == chunksize - 1:
            try:
                k = ks[i/chunksize]
            except:
                k = ks

            for inp in range (n_inputs):
                for j in range (M):
                    alphas[inp,j] += errs[inp,j] * k
                errs[inp,j] = 0

        zout[i-M/2] = z


    return zout

``

issue_title: Use assertRaises in unit tests to test exceptions
issue_body:
Currently we are using the pattern:

```python
try:
    do_something()
    self.assertFail('The above line should fail')
except SomeException, err:
    self.assertEqual(str(err), 'expected error message.')
```

to check that an exception or error is raised as expected in our unit tests.

Instead we should be using:

```python
with self.assertRaises(SomeException) as cm:
    do_something()
err = cm.exception
self.assertEqual(str(err), 'expected error message.')
```

This is the case throughout the unit test code base and should be fixed everywhere.

issue_title: Is that possible to use batchnorm for data parallelisation?
issue_body: Is it possible to make batch norm working on the whole batch when doing data split? To be more specific, batch norm calculates the mean and var for the current batch. If for example, we use batch-size=8 and use 4 gpus, then each gpu will have 2 images and the batch norm will only calculates the mean and var based on these 2. Any possibility the batch norm can work on the whole 8 images? @piiswrong @tqchen

    def __init__(self, xxx, yyy):
        self.xxx = xxx
        self.yyy = yyy
    def forward(self, is_train, req, in_data, out_data, aux):
        x = in_data[0].asnumpy()
        y = np.exp(x - x.max(axis=1).reshape((x.shape[0], 1)))
        y /= y.sum(axis=1).reshape((x.shape[0], 1))
        print(self.xxx, self.yyy)
        self.assign(out_data[0], req[0], mx.nd.array(y))

    def backward(self, req, out_grad, in_data, out_data, in_grad, aux):
        l = in_data[1].asnumpy().ravel().astype(np.int)
        y = out_data[0].asnumpy()
        y[np.arange(l.shape[0]), l] -= 1.0
        self.assign(in_grad[0], req[0], mx.nd.array(y))

)

issue_title: speed problem in distribute training
issue_body:
## 1. In file "kvstore_dist_server.py"
Change
```
419      // TODO(mli) try to remove this CopyFrom
420     response.vals.CopyFrom(static_cast<const float*>(stored.data().dptr_), len);
```
to
```
response.vals = ps::SArray<float>(stored.data().dptr<float>(), len, false);
```

In a vgg16 training with two distribute machines (total 8 gpus),  it can accelerate **20** samples/sec. 
Is this method correct?

## 2. In file "kvstore_dist.py"
Delete the line 275 "send_buf.WaitToWrite();", can accelerate the speed with kvstore='sync_device' or 'local'.
In profile, I can find this WaitToWrite cause all the push locked until the whole backward finished.
Is this WaitToWrite necessary?

issue_title: Does mxnet have a dataiter for prediction of buckets models  ?
throughput of sparse linear classification is small with small batch size
issue_body:
## Description
For small batch, sparse linear classification uses all CPU, but throughput is small. 


## Environment info (Required)
Machine used: AWS AMI, c5.9xlarge,
steps to repro:
1. pip2 install mxnet-mkl
2. git clone mxnet
3. in directory `incubator-mxnet/example/sparse/linear_classification`, run `python2 train.py --batch-size 1`

We see throughput is around 600 samples/sec. I tried to set up things like `export OMP_NUM_THREADS=vCPUs / 2`. It seems that after setting this, the CPU usage reduces (only half of the cores are used), but the throughput is not reduced. This is even the case when I setting `OMP_NUM_THREADS=1`. 


## Question
How should I set things up to increase the throughput of the linear classfication training for a single machine with multiple cores? Or does MXNet currently not optimize in this direction (i.e. not using things like Hogwild!). Thanks in advance. 

with @lcytzk

issue_title: PUBhandler can not  be used with multiprocessing
issue_body:
I have a class 'MyLogger'  for sending messages to log server by using PUBhandler. A exception raised  when MyLogger is instanced in `LogWorker.__init__()` method (like version 1),  however, it is ok if MyLogger is instanced in  `LogWorker.log_worker()` method (version 2).   any suggestions would be appreciated.

```python
import logging
from multiprocessing import Process
import os
import random
import sys
import time

import zmq
from zmq.log.handlers import PUBHandler


class MyLogger(object):
    ''''''

    def __init__(self, port, handler=None):
        self.port = port
        self.handler = handler or self._construct_sock_handler()
        self.logger = logging.getLogger()
        self.logger.setLevel(logging.INFO)
        if not self.logger.handlers:
            self.logger.addHandler(self.handler)


    def _construct_sock_handler(self):
        context = zmq.Context()
        log_sock = context.socket(zmq.PUB)
        log_sock.connect("tcp://127.0.0.1:%i" % self.port)
        time.sleep(0.1)
        handler = PUBHandler(log_sock)
        return handler


    def get_logger(self):
        return self.logger


def sub_logger(port, level=logging.DEBUG):
    ctx = zmq.Context()
    sub = ctx.socket(zmq.SUB)
    sub.bind('tcp://127.0.0.1:%i' % port)
    sub.setsockopt(zmq.SUBSCRIBE, "")
    logging.basicConfig(level=level)

    while True:
        level, message = sub.recv_multipart()
        if message.endswith('\n'):
            # trim trailing newline, which will get appended again
            message = message[:-1]
        log = getattr(logging, level.lower())
        log(message)


class LogWorker(object):
    
    def __init__(self):
        - pass   # version 1
        + self.logger = MyLogger(port).get_logger()   # version 2

    def log_worker(self, port):
        - self.logger = MyLogger(port).get_logger() # version 1
        print "starting logger at %i with level=%s" % (os.getpid(), logging.DEBUG)

        while True:
            level = logging.INFO
            self.logger.log(level, "Hello from %i!" % os.getpid())
            time.sleep(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        n = int(sys.argv[1])
    else:
        n = 2

    port = 5555

    workers = [Process(target=LogWorker().log_worker, args=(port,)) for _ in range(n)]
    [w.start() for w in workers]

    try:
        sub_logger(port)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    finally:
        [ w.terminate() for w in workers ]
```

issue_title: Voice of America Corpus?
issue_body:
Something that I discovered a while ago working on a completely different project (teaching literacy), was a large public domain corpus which could be used for training speech recognition systems.

https://learningenglish.voanews.com/ is a public broadcasting service (a U.S. equivalent the BBC world service) has a large corpus of news articles along with human narration which can be crawled from their website.

As per U.S. copyright law, since Voice of America is a federal agency, the content created by Voice of America (which includes the news articles and narration, but not the narration) is in the public domain (see https://www.usa.gov/government-works). The Voice of America terms and conditions confirm this public domain copyright status: "All text, audio and video material produced exclusively by the Voice of America is in the public domain." (quoted from the VOA Learning English terms and conditions at https://learningenglish.voanews.com/p/6021.html).

I have previously produced tools which scrape and align text from VOA and will look into whether I can release them as open source (but I don't know if/when this will be the case). In the mean time I wanted to point this out in case it's useful to your project.

The VOA narrations are all read very clearly, targeting English Language Learners, which is not necessarily going to be representative of all TTS input. But given the size of the existing publicly available speech resources, I hope that VOA can add some useful training material.

issue_title: Exception adding worker - scheduler might be running an older version
issue_body:
I was wondering what could be causing this exception:

``` sh
Exception adding worker - scheduler might be running an older version
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "envs/bdl/lib/python2.7/site-packages/luigi/worker.py", line 301, in _add_worker
    self._scheduler.add_worker(self._id, self._worker_info)
```

when trying to do something like this:

``` python
task = RootTask()
sch = RemoteScheduler(host=host, port=port)
while not task.complete():
    w = Worker(scheduler=sch, worker_processes=workers)
    w.add(task)
    w.run()
    w.stop()
    time.sleep(10)
```

I can't seem to really figure out what's the reason. I'm running a `luigid` scheduler (same version as luigi I'm using, obviously) on the same box. `RootTask` is basically a meta-task which lists other tasks as requirements, writes a dummy text file in `run()` and has a `LocalPath` pointing to this file as `output()`. All of the downstream tasks seem to complete just fine, but the root task never gets to writing this dummy file because of the exception above, so although the task is technically done, its `task.complete()` is never true.

issue_title: Improve documentation for Configservice get_paginator()
issue_body:
Currently, configservice documentation recommends to use get_paginator() but does not provide information on where to pass **kwargs afterwards.

```
get_paginator(operation_name)
Create a paginator for an operation.

Parameters
operation_name (string) -- The operation name. This is the same name as the method name on the client. For example, if the method name is create_foo, and you'd normally invoke the operation as client.create_foo(**kwargs), if the create_foo operation can be paginated, you can use the call client.get_paginator("create_foo").
Raises OperationNotPageableError
Raised if the operation is not pageable. You can use the client.can_paginate method to check if an operation is pageable.
Return type
L{botocore.paginate.Paginator}
Returns
A paginator object.
```
ref: http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/config.html#ConfigService.Client.get_paginator

get_paginator() should provide more examples, explanation, and/or link to documentation explaining how to use subsequent paginate calls with desired kwargs. as it stands, it's not clear how to use the call that needs paginating with its original arguments -- this only advises on how to create the paginator object, which is not helpful for individuals new to boto3.

issue_title: define a boto3 exception hiearchy
issue_body:
If I got it right, `boto3` adds a level of abstraction on top of `botocore`, which should enable to code in a more simple and expressive way.

However idiomatic python prefers [EAFP](https://docs.python.org/3.5/glossary.html#term-eafp) to [LBYL](https://docs.python.org/3.5/glossary.html#term-lbyl), but I was unable to find a single reference to exceptions inside `boto3` and its documentation.

I found myself writing again and again

```python
import boto3
import botocore

b3resource = boto3.resource('s3').Bucket('some bucket')

try:
    # do something with the boto3 resource
except botocore.exceptions.ClientError:
    # fix the edge situation
```

which is ugly as sin, since there is no obvious relationship between the high level `boto3` abstractions to the low level `botocore` clients.

So I would suggest these two features.
1. Define a native `boto3` exception hierarchy which hides/encapsulates the low level `botocore` one.
2. Document the correct way of catching exceptions in `boto3`.

issue_title: Cassandra query_async wrong behavior
issue_body: When running Cassandra queries asynchronously with `query_async`, it seems that any span created after has this cassandra span as a `parent_id`. Tested with last version of the tracer. In the following example there are two cassandra asynchronous calls, followed by another span. The second cassandra call is child of the first cassandra call and then the next span is child of the second cassandra call. We were able to reproduce this behaviour with @Kyle-Verhoog with 2 failing tests: - The first one is to check that any span created after the call of the asynchronous call has this cassandra call as a parent: ``` def test_query_async_parenting(self): tracer = get_dummy_tracer() Pin.get_from(self.cluster).clone(tracer=tracer).onto(self.cluster) session = self.cluster.connect(self.TEST_KEYSPACE) writer = tracer.writer event = Event() result = [] future = session.execute_async(self.TEST_QUERY) with tracer.trace('something') as not_child: pass def callback(results): result.append(ResultSet(future, results)) event.set() future.add_callback(callback) event.wait() spans = writer.pop() print(spans) assert not_child.parent_id is None ``` Here is the result: ``` -------------------- >> begin captured stdout << --------------------- [, ] --------------------- >> end captured stdout << ---------------------- ``` - The second one, similar, with two cassandra async calls: ``` def test_query_async_double(self): def execute_fn(session, query): event = Event() event2 = Event() result = [] future = session.execute_async(query) future2 = session.execute_async(query) def callback(results): result.append(ResultSet(future, results)) event.set() def callback2(results): result.append(ResultSet(future, results)) event2.set() future.add_callback(callback) future2.add_callback(callback2) event.wait() event2.wait() return result[0] session, writer = self._traced_session() execute_fn(session, self.TEST_QUERY) spans = writer.pop() print(spans) assert spans[0].parent_id is None assert spans[1].parent_id is None ``` This error is printed during the test: `ddtrace.contrib.cassandra.session: DEBUG: traced_set_final_result was not able to get the current span from the ResponseFuture`

I am confused about the way Colander handles None values (or not handles None values) during the deserialization. For example:

```
class Person(colander.MappingSchema):
    name = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String(), missing=None)

schema = Person()
print(schema.deserialize({'name': None}))
# {'name': None}
```

In this case Colander accepts a `None` value, and keeps it because `missing` is set to `None`. This sound reasonable to me.

Another example using a sequence:

```
class NoneSequence(colander.MappingSchema):
    foo = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String())
    bar = colander.SchemaNode(
        colander.Sequence(), colander.SchemaNode(colander.String()),
        missing=None)

schema = NoneSequence()
deserialized = schema.deserialize({'foo': '1', 'bar': None})
# colander.Invalid: {'bar': '"None" is not iterable'}
```

In this case Colander does not accept None.

Yet another example using a nested schema:

```
class SchemaA(colander.MappingSchema):
    val = colander.SchemaNode(colander.String())


class SchemaB(colander.MappingSchema):
    a = SchemaA(missing=None)


class SchemaC(colander.MappingSchema):
    b = SchemaB()

schema = SchemaC()
print(schema.deserialize({"b": {"a": None}}))
# colander.Invalid: {'b.a': '"None" is not a mapping type: Does not implement dict-like functionality.'}
```

Again None is not accepted. I have some more example with nested sequences and tuples here: https://gist.github.com/tsauerwein/93c88ba3c37f936dea113a27447c0db5

What is the idea? Why does Colander accept None in some cases during the deserialization, and in some cases not? Do I have to replace all None values with `colander.null` before calling `deserialize`?

Looking the tests it seems that this is even the expected behavior:
https://github.com/Pylons/colander/blob/cb1645d/colander/tests/test_colander.py#L935
https://github.com/Pylons/colander/blob/cb1645d/colander/tests/test_colander.py#L690

issue_title: Perfomance of SDK
issue_body:
Hello.
We are working with Contentful Management API and we've implemented our own decision before release of yours. One of the essential issues was API perfomance. Now we're learn a possibility to change our solution to yours and we have a suggestion for improving.
In methods, which change state of records in Contentful at the beginning request for change is done and then the method `reload` is called. In fact, this method does additional `get` request to Contentful, which is overhead, because CM API is quite slow. I think, instead of this approach, we can reload instances, based on response of request for change, because this response consist actual version of object.

For example

```python
    def publish(self):
        """Publishes the Resource"""

        response = self._client._put(
            "{0}/published".format(
                self.__class__.base_url(self.sys['space'].id, self.sys['id']),
            ),
            {},
            headers=self._update_headers()
        )

        return self.reload(response)

# ****
    def reload(self, result):
        """Reloads the Resource"""

       if result is None: 
           result = self._client._get(
                self.__class__.base_url(
                    self.sys['space'].id,
                    self.sys['id']
                )
            )

        self._update_from_resource(result)

        return self
```

issue_title: uninitialized tchannel instance is not fork-safe
issue_body:
An uninitialized `TChannel` instance throws a future onto the ioloop, which then breaks forking.

```python
$ cat test.py
import os

from tchannel import TChannel

t = TChannel('app')

print 'forking'

if os.fork() == 0:
    t.listen()
```

After adding a print statement to `tornado.platform.kqueue:register`:

```
$ python test.py
registered a file descriptor
forking
registered a file descriptor
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 10, in <module>
    t.listen()
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/tchannel/tchannel.py", line 150, in listen
    return self._dep_tchannel.listen(port)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/tchannel/tornado/tchannel.py", line 256, in listen
    server.add_sockets(sockets)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/tcpserver.py", line 144, in add_sockets
    io_loop=self.io_loop)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/netutil.py", line 266, in add_accept_handler
    io_loop.add_handler(sock, accept_handler, IOLoop.READ)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/ioloop.py", line 704, in add_handler
    self._impl.register(fd, events | self.ERROR)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/platform/kqueue.py", line 42, in register
    self._control(fd, events, select.KQ_EV_ADD)
  File "/Users/blampe/src/tchannel-python/env/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tornado/platform/kqueue.py", line 64, in _control
    self._kqueue.control([kevent], 0)
OSError: [Errno 9] Bad file descriptor
```

The future is coming from `self.peers = PeerGroup(self)`, which initializes a `Peer` and creates a future in `self._reset_condition = Condition()`.

issue_title: Narrative docs for __traceback_supplement__ incorrect
issue_body:
The documentation [gives an example](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.exceptions/blob/master/docs/narr.rst#pydata__traceback_supplement__) where `__traceback_supplement__` is set to an instance of a class.

In [reality](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.exceptions/blob/master/src/zope/exceptions/exceptionformatter.py#L143), `__traceback_supplement__` is a tuple consisting of a constructor function followed by its arguments; the constructor is called to produce the instance. If this is not done, then an exception is raised and no supplement is formatted.

By default, this leads to a nasty traceback on stderr, with no explicit information about what went wrong. 

The [interface API docs](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.exceptions/blob/master/src/zope/exceptions/interfaces.py#L24) get this correct, so only the narrative docs need updated. The narrative doc *is* a doctest, but the actual rendering isn't tested, so this currently goes undetected; it would be nice if that could be fixed too.

issue_title: Add pyfolio/perf_attrib.py to __init__.py
Alphalens Fails on Discrete Factors
issue_body:
Factors which draw from small discrete sets (such as a buy/sell factor with -1, 0, 1) fail in alphalens. A fairly uninformative error is generated. For repro you can try running it on a factor constructed from this sentiment data. We should have a more elegant failure or some kwarg to tell alphalens the factor is discrete. I solved the issue locally by adding in a tiny amount of random noise to my factor, effectively making it continuous.

https://www.quantopian.com/data/sentdex/sentiment

issue_title: Error: failed to parse some of the extra variables.
issue_body:
I've installed the version 2.3.1 (latest at the moment), I created an extra var file, but I wasn't able to pass it or extra vars in any way.
Just for the record, from the Ansible Tower, I was able to deploy without issue.

I executed the following command, then I received the error.
```
[jenkins@server ~]$ tower-cli job launch --job-template="[test] deploy" --monitor --extra-vars="build_version: devel_718"
Error: failed to parse some of the extra variables.
variables:
{
	"build_version": "VERSION",
	"env": "test",
	"library_path": "./ansible-playbooks/playbooks/"
}
```

## Details
OS: Amazon Linux (2015.03)
Python: 2.7.9
Ansible Tower 2.1.4
Tower CLI 2.3.1


## Related errors

I received the same error with:
```
[jenkins@server ~]$ tower-cli job launch --job-template="[test] deploy" --monitor --extra-vars="@vars.yml"
```
or even with:
```
[jenkins@server ~]$ tower-cli job launch --job-template="[test] deploy" --monitor
```

## Workaround
**I downgraded to version 2.3.0 and it works well (with @filename format).**

issue_title: Do not hardcode paths
issue_body:
I am trying to package Guake 3 on NixOS but Guake hardcodes some paths, making it incompatible with Nix.

- [Checking if theme exists](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/3.0.5/guake/guake_app.py#L410) should respect `XDG_DATA_DIRS`. [This](https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/gnome-tweaks/commit/878b4c0db6b5a53c70f6844eb258d5ec48dd859c) is how we fixed it in GNOME Tweaks. => Follow up #1249
- Instead of [executing `dconf`](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/3.0.5/guake/guake_app.py#L1059), appropriate [`Gio.Settings`](https://lazka.github.io/pgi-docs/Gio-2.0/classes/Settings.html) call should be made. => Follow up #1250
- [`LOCALE_DIR`](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/3.0.5/guake/globals.py#L57) should be configurable using make flags.
- Same for other directories so that Arch need not [patch it](https://git.archlinux.org/svntogit/community.git/tree/trunk/PKGBUILD?h=packages/guake&id=2b70f1e9e4f905f713b3948407e3b71a83077d26#n30).
- pixmaps and `GtkBuilder` files should be actually installed by the `Makefile`.
- Makefile should set `--prefix` flag for [`setup.py install`](https://github.com/Guake/guake/blob/3.0.5/Makefile#L42)

Downstream PR: https://github.com/NixOS/nixpkgs/pull/38642

issue_title: Add a difference argument for compute_aggregate
add methods for reading example datasets
issue_body:
We need a way to load resources (data) for code examples. We're in a unique position in that we're a cloud-hosted service, so we can rely on having datasets hosted in a special carto account and use the already-built cartoframes machinery (`cc.read`) to pull down the data.

I propose we use `cartoframes.carto.com` (which is currently used for testing) to store the data. We can move the testing account to `cartoframes-testing.carto.com` instead.

Here's a proposed API for accomplishing this (def. open for discussion):
```python
# list datasets - returns a dataframe of dataset names, descriptions, link 
#  to where it's used (notebooks in the cartoframes repo), etc.
#  we should define this better, but something 'data library'-like for metadata
cc.example.list()
# get a visual of data before downloading
cc.example.map('great_dataset', color='colname')
# download data to a dataframe
df = cc.example.read('great_dataset')
```

## Community practices

Here are some examples of how datasets are loaded from other packages.

* `bokeh` make this work by having a data download method:
    ```python
    import bokeh
    bokeh.sampledata.download()
    ```
* `nltk` like so: http://www.nltk.org/api/nltk.html#nltk.data.load
* `scikit-learn`: http://scikit-learn.org/stable/datasets/index.html

🐲 
related #183

issue_title: wrong credentials needs better error or test
issue_body:
Trying to use a wrong username and api_key credential returns the following error when trying to write a dataframe:

`JSONDecodeError: Expecting value: line 1 column 2 (char 1)`

The error should be more specific. I believe we're getting something like a 404 missing page or an error message that simply says "an API key must be provided", and the JSON decoding isn't expecting this.

We could maybe add a test when the CartoContext class gets instantiated to make sure it's a valid username and API key combination?

issue_title: db verify deployed schema
issue_body:
I think it is necessary to have a `verify` action. This would compare the currently deployed database schema version against the version omego is pointed at.

This would be useful for a non-CI docker environment. In this environment, an implicit upgrade is not desirable should there be a version mismatch. so something like

```bash
omego db verify --dbhost "$DBHOST" --dbuser "$DBUSER" --dbname "$DBNAME"\
                --dbpass "$DBPASS" --serverdir=OMERO.server && echo "Verified version"
```

The success could be used to continue without a) installing/upgrading anything. If the version was not verified then an error exit code would be returned and potentially the output can be used to determine what to do, i.e. There is no schema deployed -> `init`, otherwise: error or `upgrade` depending on the use case.

Potentially a command which checks if any schema is installed at all could be separate which could in some use cases avoid having to parse the output at all.

issue_title: EVM: Symbolic execution on a path with multiplication on signed integer differs from concrete execution
issue_body:
### Manticore version
0e8c9b0bf2b7e9b8145254f86522867b67c8947f
Note: I am not using the last master, as I didn't update my script to change `m.SValue`

### Summary of the problem
There is a difference between the symbolic execution and the concrete execution when the execution has constraints on multiplication of signed integer.

### Step to reproduce the behavior
I am trying to test the following code:

```solidity
// bugMul.sol
contract Math{

    function mul(int a, int b) external returns(int){
        int c = a * b;  
        if(a==0) return 0;
        if(b==0) return 0;
        require(c / a == b);
        return c;
    }
}
```

I want to check that all the states that are reverted are due to an overflow (== if a state reverted it's because of an overflow).
My script is:
```python
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM
from manticore.core.smtlib import Operators, solver


m = ManticoreEVM()

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)

with open('bugMul.sol') as f:
    contract = m.solidity_create_contract(f, owner=owner_account)


contract.mul(m.SValue, m.SValue)

# from https://github.com/trailofbits/manticore/blob/master/manticore/ethereum.py
def signed_mul_overflow(a, b):
    '''
    Sign extend the value to 512 bits and check the result can be represented
     in 256. Following there is a 32 bit excerpt of this condition:
    a  *  b           +00000000000000000 +00000000000000001 +0000000003fffffff +0000000007fffffff +00000000080000001 +000000000bfffffff +000000000ffffffff
    +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000000
    +0000000000000001  +0000000000000000  +0000000000000001  +000000003fffffff  +000000007fffffff  +0000000080000001  +00000000bfffffff  +00000000ffffffff
    +000000003fffffff  +0000000000000000  +000000003fffffff *+0fffffff80000001 *+1fffffff40000001 *+1fffffffbfffffff *+2fffffff00000001 *+3ffffffec0000001
    +000000007fffffff  +0000000000000000  +000000007fffffff *+1fffffff40000001 *+3fffffff00000001 *+3fffffffffffffff *+5ffffffec0000001 *+7ffffffe80000001
    +0000000080000001  +0000000000000000  +0000000080000001 *+1fffffffbfffffff *+3fffffffffffffff *+4000000100000001 *+600000003fffffff *+800000007fffffff
    +00000000bfffffff  +0000000000000000  +00000000bfffffff *+2fffffff00000001 *+5ffffffec0000001 *+600000003fffffff *+8ffffffe80000001 *+bffffffe40000001
    +00000000ffffffff  +0000000000000000  +00000000ffffffff *+3ffffffec0000001 *+7ffffffe80000001 *+800000007fffffff *+bffffffe40000001 *+fffffffe00000001
    '''
    mul = Operators.SEXTEND(a, 256, 512) * Operators.SEXTEND(b, 256, 512)
    cond = Operators.OR(mul < -(1 << 255), mul >= (1 << 255))
    return cond

print "run checks"

# Check that all the reverted state are due to an overflow
for state in m.terminated_states:
    # Retrieve inputs
    data_mul = state.platform.transactions[1].data
    x = Operators.CONCAT(256, *data_mul[4:36])
    y = Operators.CONCAT(256, *data_mul[36:68])
    input_mul = x * y

    # check for overflow
    with state as tmp_state:

        # Add the constraint that there is no overflow, if it's SAT, a bug is found
        tmp_state.constrain(Operators.NOT(signed_mul_overflow(x, y)))

        if solver.check(tmp_state.constraints):
            # Get the values, one by one
            x_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, x)
            tmp_state.constrain(x == x_solved)

            y_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, y)
            tmp_state.constrain(y == y_solved)

            # As the path reverted, z is computed from the input
            z_solved = solver.get_value(tmp_state.constraints, input_mul)

            print "Correct mul found in reverted states!, %x %x %x"%(x_solved, y_solved, z_solved)

            # Generate a testcase file 
            m.generate_testcase(tmp_state, 'BugMul')
            print "See "+m.workspace
```

It found a reverted path for which there is no overflow. For example:
```
Function call:
mul(1393966716111906825181365749768185729516032L,20766652442136795645389052729894022L) -> REVERT (*)
```

When I execute the same contract with the concrete input provided by Manticore, I obtain a different path (which does not revert):
```python
from manticore.ethereum import ManticoreEVM

m = ManticoreEVM()

owner_account = m.create_account(balance=1000)

with open('bugMul.sol') as f:
    contract = m.solidity_create_contract(f, owner=owner_account)


contract.mul(0x100080000100000000000000000000000200, 0x3ffe000ffc3360000000e00702486)

print m.workspace

m.finalize()
```
Gives:
```
Function call:
mul(1393966716111906825181365749768185729516032L,20766652442136795645389052729894022L) -> RETURN

issue_title: schema built when creating a view in bigquery
Explore going the route of using Clients ?
issue_body:
After our last talk, there have been quite a few different ideas tossed around to make it clear and obvious which credentials and project IDs are in use during a particular API call, some of those have been....

1. Changing the default values globally
1. Making "Connections" a context manager (`with connection: # do something`)
1. Creating the concept of a client

We do (1), are talking about doing (2), while the others tend to do (3) -- and comparing the code, I think (3) is the nicest. 

gcloud-node:

```javascript
var gcloud = require('gcloud');
var project1_storage = gcloud.storage({projectId: 'project1', keyFilename: '/path/to/key'});
var project2_storage_auto = gcloud.storage();

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.get_bucket('bucket-1')
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.get_bucket('bucket2')
```

gcloud-ruby

```ruby
require 'gcloud/storage'
project1_storage = Gcloud.storage "project-id-1" "/path/to/key"
project2_storage_auto = Gcloud.storage  # Magically figure out the project ID and credentials

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.find_bucket "bucket-1"
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.find_bucket "bucket-2"
```

gcloud-python

```python
from gcloud import storage
from gcloud.credentials import get_for_service_account_json

# Create two different credentials.
credentials1 = get_for_service_account_json('key1.json')
credentials2 = get_for_service_account_json('key2.json')

# Create two different connections.
connection1 = storage.Connection(credentials=credentials1)
connection2 = storage.Connection(credentials=credentials2)

# Get two different buckets
bucket1 = storage.get_bucket('bucket-1', project='project1', connection=connection1)
bucket2 = storage.get_bucket('bucket-2', project='project2', connection=connection2)
```

gcloud-python if we followed the client pattern:

```python
from gcloud import storage

project1_storage = storage.Client('project1', '/path/to/key')
project2_storage_auto = storage.Client()

# Do things with the two "clients"...
bucket1 = project1_storage.get_bucket('bucket-1')
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.get_bucket('bucket-2')
```

Another option for gcloud-python using the client pattern:

```python
import gcloud

project1_storage = gcloud.storage('project1', '/path/to/key')
project2_storage_auto = gcloud.storage()

# Do things with the two "clients"
bucket1 = project1_storage.get_bucket('bucket-1')
bucket2 = project2_storage_auto.get_bucket('bucket-2')
```

/cc @dhermes @tseaver

issue_title: Running and bundling tests with namespace packages
issue_body:
(Context: PR #2223 towards #1978)

PR #2223's tests can't run because nose will place `cwd` into `sys.path` when running tests. This means `import google.cloud` will import `./google/cloud` instead of `{env}/lib/python{x}.{y}/site-packages/google/cloud`. This will clobber all other `google` namespace packages. There is an long-open issue [here](https://github.com/nose-devs/nose/issues/856), note that Nose has been largely abandoned.

There are a two of ways we could resolve this, and maybe we should do both:

1. Move the tests to their own top-level package as described in the [py.test docs](http://doc.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html#choosing-a-test-layout-import-rules).
1. Switch to py.test's test runner, `pytest`. It's fully compatible with nose/unittest style tests. py.test appropriately deals with namespace/installed packages.

## On switching to py.test

1. py.test has slightly different arguments than nose, notably for coverage. This is pretty easy to deal with.
1. Switching to py.test will either mean we have to switch to having the tests in a separate package *or* that we have to switch from tox to [nox](https://nox.readthedocs.io). The reason is that py.test will try to import relative to the test modules it discovers, and so will try to import the module-under-test from the current working directory, but will actually give an error because the same module is present in `site-packages`. This can be resolved by either moving the tests to a separate package or installing the package in "editable" mode with `pip install -e`. Unfortunately, tox doesn't easily allow you to change the command used to install the package under test. There might be a few hacks (turning off sdist and manually installing the package). Nox however gives you complete control over the creation, [here's a simple config](https://gist.github.com/jonparrott/0b2873711e49ab07fdac59b9c5e640c6) that works for gcloud-python.

## On moving the tests to a test package

The were originally concerns about having the tests in a separate top-level tests package because we didn't want it to get installed with gcloud-python but we did want to distribute the tests. This should be resolvable by telling `find_packages()` to skip `tests` and including `tests` in `manifest.in`. This means that wheels and sdists will contain the tests folder for downstream packagers, but pip will not install the tests package into `site-packages`.

issue_title: When sources is a string, provide a good error message
issue_body:
Currently, we don't check for str, so when `create_sources_field` calls `Files.create_fileset_with_spec(sources_rel_path, *sources)`, it splits the string into chars. If the string has no `.`s, it'll just point to a bunch of one letter files that don't exist. If it does have a `.`, eg `my_pkg.py`, then it will blow up with an error like:

```
Exception message: [Errno 21] Is a directory: u'<path-to-BUILD-file>/.'
For reference, simplified version of BUILD file:
some_target(name='whatever',
  sources='should-be-a-list.py'
)
```

issue_title: Add variable interpolation for the distribution picked into the jvm platform argumetns
issue_body:
We are currently using pants 0.0.53


Here's what we currently do to specify bootclasspath:

```
[jvm-platform]
default_platform: 1.8
platforms: {
    '1.7': {'source': '1.7', 'target': '1.7', 'args': ["-C-Xbootclasspath:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/resources.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/rt.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/sunrsasign.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jsse.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jce.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/charsets.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jfr.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/classes", "-C-Xlint:cast", "-C-Xlint:deprecation", "-C-Xlint:empty", "-C-Xlint:finally"] },
    '1.8': {'source': '1.8', 'target': '1.8', 'args': ["-C-Xbootclasspath:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/resources.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/rt.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/sunrsasign.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jsse.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jce.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/charsets.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/lib/jfr.jar:/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home/jre/classes", "-C-Xlint:cast", "-C-Xlint:deprecation", "-C-Xlint:empty", "-C-Xlint:finally"] },
  }

[jvm-distributions]
paths: {
    "mac os x": ["/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.8.0_45.jdk/Contents/Home", "/Library/Java/JavaVirtualMachines/jdk1.7.0_80.jdk/Contents/Home"],
    "linux": ["/usr/java/jdk1.8.0_45", "/usr/java/jdk1.7.0_80"],
  }
```

In practice, we generate this file. Currently , it generates linux paths for `-Xbootclasspath:` on Linux and mac paths when you exec pants on a mac.  What would be better is the ability to do some kind of string interpolation inside one of the 'args' variables for the `platforms` setting.

Something like:
```
[jvm-platform]
default_platform: 1.8
platforms: {
    '1.7': {'source': '1.7', 'target': '1.7', 'args': ["-C-Xbootclasspath:(%distribution)/jre/lib/resources.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/rt.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/sunrsasign.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/jsse.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/jce.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/charsets.jar:(%distribution)/jre/lib/jfr.jar:(%distribution)/jre/classes", "-C-Xlint:cast", "-C-Xlint:deprecation", "-C-Xlint:empty", "-C-Xlint:finally"] },
...
```

issue_title: Explore more efficient analysis format for pants internal usage
issue_body:
This is a follow up task for #3962 .

Analysis files are used by
* zinc itself for incremental compile
* pants for products and dependencies

Right now they share the same plain text file, but they don't have to.

zinc can use the zip file like upstream does, which may have performance benefit when files are large and many.

pants can use a more compact internal representation derived from the former, this perhaps is necessary since we need a place for the reconstructed inheritance dependencies.

issue_title: Help printer doesn't exit 1 on failure
issue_body: `src/python/pants/help/help_printer.py` will not always exit 1 for unknown goals. In particular, this caused a set of failing tests to go unnoticed for a while due to string concatenation of their first two arguments: https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/2859/

issue_title: wiki url builder is not actually passed a target and config dict
issue_body:
The [BUILD Dictionary](https://www.pantsbuild.org/build_dictionary.html) specifies that the `url_builder` parameter given to the `Wiki` is a **"Function that accepts a page target and an optional wiki config dict"**. This is inline with the confluence publishing information given on the [markdown page](https://www.pantsbuild.org/page.html#publishing). However, after trying to follow this example, and looking into the [source](https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/1.3.x/src/python/pants/backend/docgen/tasks/markdown_to_html.py#L155) it appears that this is not true, and the example is therefore incorrect and unusable.

I'd recommend a fix myself but haven't had the time to dig into the surrounding processes at all... 

Cheers!
